questions.question: Define a binary tree and state two properties that distinguish a strict (full) binary tree from a general binary tree.
keyPoints: Binary tree: each node has at most two children, called left and right.
In a strict binary tree, every internal node has exactly two children.
Number of leaf nodes = number of internal nodes + 1 in a strict binary tree.
A strict binary tree can be perfect but a perfect tree is a special case of strict.

questions.question: Explain the difference between the depth and the level of a node in a binary tree. How is height of the tree defined?
keyPoints: Depth: number of edges from root to the node; root depth = 0.
Level: often used interchangeably with depth, but can also denote the layer starting at 1 (root at level 1).
Height of a tree: number of edges on the longest downward path from root to a leaf; a single node has height 0.
Depth and level refer to the same concept but differ in whether the root is counted as 0 or 1.

questions.question: Given a binary tree with n nodes, prove that in a full (strict) binary tree the number of leaf nodes L equals the number of internal nodes I + 1. Then derive the formula for the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h and state the condition under which this maximum is attained (i.e., a perfect binary tree).
keyPoints: In a full binary tree each internal node has exactly two children, so total edges = n - 1; counting leaf and internal nodes gives L = I + 1.
Maximum nodes = 2^{h+1} - 1 when height h (root at height 0) is used; equivalently 2^{h} - 1 if height is defined as number of levels minus one.
A perfect binary tree achieves this bound: all internal nodes have two children and all leaves are at the same depth.
Example: a perfect binary tree of height 2 has 2^{3} - 1 = 7 nodes.

questions.question: Describe two common ways to represent a binary tree in memory (linked representation using nodes with left and right pointers, and sequential/array representation). For a specific binary tree with at least 5 nodes, illustrate both representations and show how an inorder traversal would be performed on each.
keyPoints: Linked representation: each node contains data, left child pointer, right child pointer; null pointers for missing children.
Sequential representation: store nodes in an array; for index i, left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, parent at floor((i-1)/2).
Inorder traversal: recursively visit left subtree, visit node, then right subtree; order depends on representation (recursive for linked, iterative index handling for array).
Example tree: root A, left child B (with children D, E), right child C (with child F); show array layout and recursive calls.
